creativeNotes: Strong episode — this is the show operating at its best. The five escalating choices give the branching real dramatic logic instead of feeling like a checklist: each one raises the stakes established by the last (small favor → private disclosure → public defense → public self-disclosure → private reckoning) before culminating in the unforced lunch-table choice that the whole season has been building toward. Alex's arc lands with real weight — the Kettering River detail returning in presentation day and again in the branch endings is a genuinely lovely piece of craft, giving the audience payoff for paying attention. Maya continues to avoid being a cardboard mean girl; her crack in scene-11a ('If people stop laughing at my jokes, they stop looking at me') is earned by everything we already know about her arc potential, and it doesn't excuse her behavior so much as contextualize it. Wren's internal lines do a lot of heavy lifting efficiently ('No system for this part. Guess I'm making one up as I go.') without over-explaining the theme. The three-branch outcome structure (authentic/safe/reckoning) respects the player enough to let 'the performance holds' be a legitimate, non-punished ending rather than a moralized bad ending — that's a mature choice for a coming-of-age game.

story: The five-choice structure is well-paced, but scene-8's presentation day compresses a lot (Alex's speech, Wren's internal dread, and the pivot to her own turn) into very few beats — consider one more breath/reaction beat between Alex sitting down and Wren being called on, so the audience feels the seconds ticking the way Wren would.

Scene-10 (hallway) resolves quickly into choice-5 — Maya lingering silently is a nice visual cue, but a single additional line of internal monologue from Wren noticing that lingering would sharpen the stakes of the choice before the player commits.
character: Jordan is consistently the conflict-avoidant peacemaker across every scene he's in, which is correct to his profile — but he never gets a single moment of his own cost the way Maya and Alex do this episode; that's fine for this installment given his arcPotential is slated for later, just flag it doesn't need fixing here.

Mr. Alvarez's 'DAYS SINCE INCIDENT' whiteboard detail and his own outsider history are set up in the character bible but never surface even obliquely in this episode — a missed chance for a one-line callback (e.g., him hesitating after Alex's presentation) that would deepen the parallel the bible promises for later episodes. Not a defect, just a note for whoever writes his next appearance.
dialogue: Maya's 'Table's still open, by the way. Door's not locked or whatever' is a great line — consistent with her established voice guidelines and does double duty as both retreat and olive branch.

Alex's 'I like things that come back even when they wreck everything first' is doing a lot of thematic work very economically — good restraint not to have Wren comment on it in dialogue and let it just land.
tone: The safe-branch ending (branch-safe) is appropriately melancholic without being punishing — 'Nobody at this table knows what a Kettering River is' is the right note: costly but not catastrophic, consistent with the show's grounded-realism tone.
Good restraint keeping Maya's cruelty at 'cutting' rather than 'vicious' in scene-6 — stays true to the world's tone of grounded realism rather than tipping into after-school-special villainy.

inspirationReferences.title: Eighth Grade
inspirationReferences.reasoning: Shares this episode's commitment to the specific, unglamorous texture of early-teen social performance and the exhausting effort of trying to seem fine.

inspirationReferences.title: PEN15
inspirationReferences.reasoning: Similar tonal balance of humor and real emotional stakes in middle-school social dynamics, and a similarly generous treatment of the 'mean girl' as a scared kid rather than a villain.

inspirationReferences.title: El Deafo by Cece Bell
inspirationReferences.reasoning: Alex's invented-world notebook as both shield and eventual bridge to connection echoes this book's use of a private creative outlet as a stand-in for the vulnerability its protagonist can't voice directly.
